How Economic Uncertainty is Reshaping Hiring Strategies

Economic uncertainty is forcing companies to rethink how they hire, shifting from aggressive expansion to more cautious, strategic workforce planning. Instead of mass hiring, organizations are focusing on quality over quantity—prioritizing multi-skilled professionals who can adapt quickly to changing business needs. Contract roles, freelancing, and project-based hiring are rising as businesses aim to reduce long-term financial commitments while staying flexible. There’s also a stronger emphasis on productivity, digital skills, and ROI-driven roles, especially in areas like AI, data, and automation. At the same time, hiring processes are becoming more data-driven, with companies using analytics to make smarter talent decisions. In uncertain times, resilience, adaptability, and continuous learning are becoming the most valued traits in candidates.

Biggest Tech Hiring Challenge in 2026?

Everyone talks about hiring problems but the reality is, it’s not just one issue anymore.
It’s a mix of four major pressures shaping every hiring decision today:

• Talent Shortage
Great developers exist, but the right ones are harder to find than ever. Specialized skills, real-world experience, and problem-solving ability are becoming non-negotiable.

• Budget Constraints
Companies want top-tier talent, but budgets aren’t scaling at the same pace. This gap is forcing smarter hiring models, remote teams, outsourcing, and flexible contracts.

• Retention
Hiring is only half the battle. Keeping skilled developers engaged in a competitive, opportunity-rich market is where most companies struggle.

• AI Disruption
AI isn’t replacing developers - but it’s redefining what “valuable talent” looks like. The bar is higher now: adaptability, AI fluency, and strategic thinking matter more than ever.
The companies winning in 2026 aren’t just hiring faster, they’re hiring smarter.

Which of these is your biggest challenge right now? 👇

Certification vs Experience: What U.S. Tech Recruiters Actually Prefer in 2026
After placing hundreds of tech professionals this year, here's the straight talk from the trenches:
✅ Experience wins for proven delivery, Real-world projects, problem-solving under pressure, and the ability to ship code/features that drive business results still matter most. Recruiters and hiring managers repeatedly say: "Certs get you in the door, but experience gets you the offer."
✅ Certifications are powerful accelerators, Especially in cloud (AWS, Azure), cybersecurity, and AI. They:

Help career switchers and juniors stand out
Signal continuous learning and up-to-date skills
Are often required or heavily preferred in regulated or fast-evolving fields

The 2026 reality? Hybrid profiles dominate. Candidates who combine solid hands-on experience with relevant, vendor-recognized certifications (AWS Solutions Architect, CompTIA Security+, Azure, etc.) get interviews faster and command stronger rates.
Skills-based hiring is growing, but "skills + proof of application" beats theory every time.

The goal for HTC Consulting is to do $1M a year in revenue. To hit that I need to become more AI native.

I want to get there without burning out or over hiring, so I have to think about growth through workflows instead of headcount. 

That's why I'm going deep on automation right now and I want to document it to share with whoever is out there watching.

Here's where I'm focused and where you could learn:

→ Client onboarding is the first one. 

When a contract gets signed I want Cowork/CC to automatically spin up a client folder with scripts, ICP docs, SOPs, and system access. I want little to no manual setup or botched handoffs. 

I want my client to feel the professionalism on day one before I've even dialled.

→ Pre-call research is the second one. Before I hop on a discovery call with a high value prospect, I punch in their name and domain and a workflow spits back research, talking points, and a tailored script. Right now that's semi-manual because the research isn't up to my standards, my fault there though.

→ End of day reporting is the third one. 

Automated insights, red flags, and summaries so nothing falls through the cracks across multiple campaigns and callers.

The reason I'm investing in this now is simple is super simple:

Every hour I spend on admin, onboarding, and reporting is an hour I'm not spending on the three things that actually move me to that $1M mark:

→ Getting new clients 

→ Upselling and retaining existing ones 

→ Coaching and developing my callers as well as myself

I brought on a VA recently and it exposed every gap in my process. 

Once I brought someone else on and they have to follow your system, every bottleneck pops out immediately.

It's painful right now but it's good.

→ Ignorance is debt:

Every hour I don't have a system for something is an hour I'm paying interest on later. 

That's why I'm investing in more coaching right now, even if it costs a few grand, the ROI is stupid if it buys back 10 hours a week. I'll keep you guys posted on it as I'm really excited about upleveling myself.

Another important thing that I think Is a moat for me is talent attraction, I get great cold callers/SDRs/SDR leaders hitting me up regularly wanting to come stack up some contracts and eventually come FT, I have lil to no cost for talent.

That's how I'm thinking about this. This is the lever that lets me stay focused on revenue and making clients more happier.

Contract labor can protect access in the short term. It cannot carry the workforce model forever.

That is the uncomfortable truth many healthcare organizations are already living. The American Hospital Association reported that hospitals’ labor costs increased by more than $42.5 billion between 2021 and 2023 to reach $839 billion, accounting for nearly 60% of the average hospital’s expenses. It also reported that hospitals spent approximately $51.1 billion on contracted staff in 2023 to fill gaps and maintain access to care. Those figures make it clear that contract labor is not being used as a small tactical supplement. It has become a costly response to structural instability in the workforce system. (American Hospital Association)

That does not mean contract labor is unnecessary. In many environments it is an essential shock absorber. It keeps units open, fills sudden gaps, supports seasonal pressure, and gives organizations time they might not otherwise have. But that is exactly the point: it buys time. It does not solve the underlying reason that time had to be bought in the first place.
The market should be more candid about that.

Heavy reliance on premium staffing often signals deeper flaws—slow permanent hiring, fragmented workflows, poor candidate movement, weak readiness design, or high preventable turnover. Contract labor then becomes the visible cost of invisible inefficiency.

That’s why it’s dangerous to mistake the shock absorber for the engine. A stronger strategy asks: how often must we rely on premium labor? The answer usually lies upstream—faster permanent hiring, better recruiter leverage, smoother candidate movement, earlier trust and readiness handling, and lower workflow friction.

This is where Gigin Health fits more naturally than a generic AI hiring tool would. Its positioning is built around connected healthcare hiring operations: a healthcare ATS plus agentic suite, Gia handling front-end recruiting load, a workflow from application to onboarding, and healthcare-specific support for repetitive tasks and credential-oriented process steps. In practical terms, that points toward exactly the kind of workflow compression organizations need if they want to reduce reliance on high-cost labor substitutes over time. (gigin.health)

The strategic goal is not to eliminate contract labor completely. That would be unrealistic. The goal is to make it less necessary as a response to preventable process weakness.
Contract labor should remain a tactical instrument. It should not become the business model the hiring system quietly depends on.

Healthcare organizations and staffing teams looking to strengthen permanent hiring workflows and reduce avoidable labor friction can explore Gigin.Health via gigin.ai or write to info@gigin.ai.

The hottest legal career of the decade nobody is talking about.

AI is writing contracts. Governments are drafting emergency laws overnight. And companies are being fined billions  because nobody on their legal team understood data.

That career gap? It's your opportunity.

AI Governance & Data Privacy Law is no longer a niche. It is the fastest-growing legal specialty on the planet and the lawyers who master it now will set the rules everyone else follows for the next 30 years.

Here is your exact roadmap:

1. Master the foundational law first.
GDPR, CCPA, DPDP Act (India), EU AI Act read the primary texts, not just summaries. You cannot advise on what you have not read.

2. Learn how AI actually works.
You do not need to code. But you must understand training data, model bias, automated decision-making, and LLMs well enough to draft governance policies around them.

3. Get certified strategically.
CIPP/E, CIPM from IAPP are the gold standard. Add the PECB ISO/IEC 42001 AI Management certification. Stack credentials that signal you speak both legal and technical.

4. Build a visible niche.
Write on LinkedIn. Publish analysis of new AI regulations. Comment on enforcement actions. Your expertise is invisible until you show it publicly.

5. Position inside or outside a firm.
In-house tech companies, Big Law tech practices, and boutique privacy firms are all hiring. Regulators and governments are hiring too and they pay more than people think.

6. Join the right communities.
IAPP, Future of Privacy Forum, AI Now Institute. Relationships in this field move faster than job boards. The next opportunity will come from a conversation, not a listing.

The lawyers who shaped internet law in the 1990s did not wait for a perfect syllabus. They learned as the law was being written. That window is open again right now, in AI.

You do not need a computer science degree. You do not need 10 years of Big Law experience. You need curiosity, a willingness to read everything, and the discipline to show up before it is obvious.

If this helped you, repost it for a lawyer in your network who needs to see this.

And drop a comment what part of AI law are you most curious about? I read every reply.

BPOs aren't being disrupted by AI. They're being erased.

Disruption implies a vendor refresh — a new player wins share from incumbents. Same pie, different slices.

What's happening to the $300B BPO industry is different. Klarna replaced 700 customer service agents with one AI system handling 2.3M conversations in its first month. That's not a vendor change. That's a category collapse.

The math is brutal. A BPO seat in Manila costs $12-18/hour fully loaded and scales linearly. An AI agent costs cents per resolution and scales infinitely — zero hiring lag, zero attrition, zero shift management. The unit economics don't compete. They obsolete.

Procurement leads who renewed five-year BPO contracts in 2024 are now looking at AI-native operators delivering the same SLA at 80% lower cost. The question stops being "which BPO" and becomes "do we still need one."

McKinsey estimates 60-70% of BPO-handled tasks are automatable with current LLM capabilities. Sequoia called AI services "the largest TAM in software history" precisely because it cannibalises the labour line, not the software line.

The BPO stack isn't being upgraded. It's being replaced by an AI workforce layer that ships outcomes, not headcount.

We are missing the point on AI in healthcare.

Everyone is fixated on hospitals while the backbone of our health system, clinics, are an afterthought. That is not just an oversight. It is a strategic mistake.

Flip through any article on AI in Canadian healthcare and you will see the same narrative. Ambient scribes in academic medical centers. Clinical AI agents rolling out across regional health systems. Vital work. But it completely bypasses where the vast majority of Canadians actually get their care.

Over 80% of patient interactions happen outside hospital walls. Family medicine clinics. Dental practices. Specialist offices. Walk-ins. Care homes. Organizations on razor-thin margins, staff stretched to their limits, and zero IT departments.

The AI solutions built for health systems simply do not fit here. Enterprise contracts worth $50,000 or more with six-month implementations are designed for procurement departments and IT teams. A solo dentist is not hiring a CIO. A care home director on a government per-diem does not have a tech staff.

So we made a conscious choice. Start where decisions move fast. Hospital procurement goes on 12 to 18 months. The organizations we serve decide in weeks. That speed lets us build the data, case studies, and integrations that will open hospital doors far more effectively than knocking on them cold today.

Everyone is chasing the hospital contract. We are serving the organizations they overlooked on the way in. And building the proof that gets us inside.

If you run a clinic in Canada, what is the one thing you wish technology could take off your plate tomorrow?

I just spent a week visiting clinics in Bangalore, India. Not as a patient. As a PM trying to understand a system I keep hearing is "broken."

A few things I didn't expect / Lessons Learnt :

The most valuable metric for a clinic owner isn't revenue or NPS. It's patient turnover = appointments per doctor per day. Everything else is downstream of this number. And almost no software is built around it.

Doctors who own their clinics are nearly impossible to schedule with. They're seeing patients back-to-back. So when you walk in for a "demo," you're competing with their actual job.

Patients will wait. For hours. First-come-first-served is still the default in most clinics. The "appointment" is more of a soft suggestion than a contract. Which means the entire patient experience is shaped by uncertainty, not by the doctor.

Practo shows doctors their average consult time. Start to end, per patient. Clinic owners told me this is one of the features they value most.

When you're building for an environment where the operator is too busy to talk to you, the patient is too anxious to give clean feedback, and the workflow runs on memory and improvisation. The PM playbook from B2B SaaS doesn't transfer cleanly.

You can't run a discovery call. You sit in the waiting room. You can't ship and iterate based on dashboards. You ride along with the receptionist for a day. You can't use synthetic users. The real users are right there, and they're scared.

I'll be sharing more from these visits over the next few weeks.

If you're building anything in healthcare ops, primary care, or for clinics, would love to compare notes. DMs open!

With Q2 now underway, the market is clearly experiencing more cautious hiring behaviour, driven by continued macroeconomic uncertainty. At the same time, technology and AI remain firmly at the forefront, reshaping both recruitment processes and the wider HR function. In Q1 and into Q2, we've observed a noticeable shift in client hiring strategies, with a growing preference for temporary and contract appointments over permanent hires. 

The rollout of the Employment Rights Bill this month has added further complexity, making this a particularly busy and challenging period for HR professionals. Earlier in 2026, we also saw a notable increase in Employee Relations-focused roles, as organisations respond to a rise in workplace cases and the growing need for specialist support in this area. 

Another emerging trend is the gradual return to increased office attendance. While still in its early stages for many organisations, it will be interesting to see how this develops and what longer-term impact it has on attraction, retention, and overall hiring strategies. 

From a candidate perspective, application volumes have risen significantly in Q1. However, this has been accompanied by a wider variation in application quality, with more candidates taking a speculative approach to applications. As a result, hiring processes are becoming more time-intensive, with increased emphasis on effective screening and shortlisting. 

Overall, the market remains active but measured. While opportunities continue to exist, both clients and candidates are approaching decisions with greater caution, creating a more considered and competitive hiring landscape as we move through Q2.

𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐰𝐚𝐲 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐡𝐢𝐫𝐞 𝐢𝐬 𝐚𝐛𝐨𝐮𝐭 𝐭𝐨 𝐛𝐞𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐛𝐢𝐠𝐠𝐞𝐬𝐭 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐞𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐚𝐝𝐯𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐠𝐞  𝐨𝐫 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐜𝐨𝐬𝐭𝐥𝐢𝐞𝐬𝐭 𝐛𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐝 𝐬𝐩𝐨𝐭.

Most business owners are still making a 1985 hiring decision in a 2025 economy.
Here is what the data, the history, and the future are telling you and why it matters now more than ever.

𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐭𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞

𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐏𝐚𝐬𝐭
The traditional employee was the only model. You hired in-person, paid fixed salaries, provided office space, and absorbed overhead costs regardless of output.

𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐏𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭
Virtual assistants now handle everything from calendar management to customer service, social media, research, and bookkeeping  remotely, on demand, at a fraction of the cost. 

𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐅𝐮𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞
By 2030, analysts project that up to 50% of business support functions will be handled by remote or AI assisted virtual workers. The question is no longer if your business will adapt  it is whether you will lead that adaptation or be forced into it.

78% of VAs report higher productivity than in office counterparts (Upwork, 2023)
$11K average annual savings per VA vs. a full-time traditional hire (Forbes)
59% of SMEs globally now use at least one virtual assistant (Statista, 2024)

So what is the real difference?

A traditional employee offers depth, company culture integration, and long-term institutional knowledge. They are essential for leadership, operations management, and roles that demand physical presence or deep team collaboration.

A virtual assistant offers flexibility, cost efficiency, and scalability. They are ideal for recurring tasks, administrative support, and specialized project-based work without the overhead of benefits, office space, or fixed contracts.

The smartest business owners are not choosing one over the other. They are asking a smarter question: Which tasks require human presence, and which require human skill?

India's Top 5 IT companies had just  “17 Net Additions” in last 9 months . It misses the Real Story of India’s IT Hiring

While the number is factually correct, it is not the right metric to judge the industry’s hiring health.

Here’s why the narrative is incomplete.

1. Net Addition ≠ Total Hiring
These companies together employ ~1.53 million people.  
With 12–14% attrition, nearly 1.5–2 lakh employees leave and get replaced every year.

Even if we assume a conservative 10% attrition, that’s still 150,000 people moving out and being backfilled.

2. Hiring Has Shifted, Not Stopped. The mix has changed dramatically:

- More niche skill hiring (cloud, cybersecurity, data, GenAI)  
- Fewer generic IT roles  
- More lateral replacements instead of bulk fresher hiring  
- Bench optimization to improve margins  
- Utilization at multi‑year highs

This reduces visible headcount growth but not the actual hiring activity.

3. Subcontracting & Gig Workforce Are Growing

A significant share of delivery today happens through:

- Contract staffing  
- Partner vendors  
- Project‑based gig roles  
- Offshore/nearshore micro‑teams

These workers do not appear in the net addition numbers.

4. Global Capacity Centers - GCCs Are Absorbing a Huge Chunk of Tech Hiring
India now has 1,600+ GCCs, and they are hiring aggressively in:

- Product engineering  
- AI/ML  
- Cloud  
- Cybersecurity  
- Platform modernization

Many roles that once went to IT services are now going to GCCs.  
This is a redistribution, not a collapse.

5. Automation Reduces Incremental Hiring, Not Replacement Hiring
GenAI, low‑code, and automation reduce the need for additional headcount.  
But companies still need to:

- Replace attrition  
- Staff new projects  
- Build new capability teams  
- Support global clients

Hiring continues—just in different pockets.


6. The “1.5M Engineering Graduates” Comparison Is Misleading
The annual engineering output includes:

- Non‑CS branches  
- Tier 3/4 colleges  
- Students needing significant upskilling  
- Graduates not seeking IT jobs at all

Comparing this number to IT net additions is not apples-to-apples.

7. Profit Growth Doesn’t Always Mean Headcount Growth
IT services profitability today is driven by:

- Higher utilization  
- Pyramid restructuring  
- Automation  
- Reduced bench  
- Offshore leverage  
- Pricing discipline

These levers improve margins without increasing headcount.

The industry is not shrinking.  
It is transforming—in skill mix, delivery models, and workforce structure.

Net additions alone cannot capture the complexity of a 1.5 million–strong industry undergoing a structural shift.
GCCs, automation, and niche‑skill demand are redefining how and where hiring happens. Net additions capture only the surface — the real movement is underneath. To understand the industry today, we need to look beyond headcount and focus on capability, value, and transformation.

Upwork just made a big move, Uma is now a selectable option right from the start.

When you create a new job post on Upwork, you'll now see Uma as a job type option at the very beginning. AI-powered hiring isn't buried in the background anymore, it's front and center.

And here's the real shift — Upwork is now a PPC + AI game.
Freelancers who aren't adapting to this new reality are already falling behind. Visibility on Upwork is no longer just about having a great profile. It's about understanding how Pay-Per-Click (Connects) and AI-driven selection work together. Uma decides who gets shortlisted, and PPC determines who gets seen. If you're not optimizing for both, you're invisible.

But here's the elephant in the room — why aren't clients hiring?

It's the fees. Plain and simple.
Before, client fees were around 5%. With VAT, card processing fees, and bank charges, clients were paying roughly 8-10% per job to Upwork. That was already painful but some manageable.

Now? Client fees have doubled to 10% for business plan. Add VAT, credit card fees, state taxes, transaction charges, and contract initiation fees on top of that and clients are now paying nearly 15-18% of every job to Upwork.

Let that sink in. For every $1,000 project, almost $180 goes to platform fees just client alone.

These fees are becoming unbearable for clients. And the result?
Upwork quietly shifted their giant, high-value clients to their new enterprise platform, Lifted. What's left on the main Upwork marketplace? Budget clients, low-quality jobs, and a race to the bottom.

The premium clients are gone. The big contracts are gone. And freelancers are left fighting over scraps with inflated Connect costs while clients struggle to justify the fees.

Meanwhile, Uma is doing this behind the scenes:
It scans 2.2 million+ active freelancer profiles, reasons through job requirements using agentic AI, sends invites to the top 20 matches, and ranks all proposals to surface a Top 5 shortlist.

The ranking is based on skill relevance, work history, Job Success Score, and alignment with must-have requirements, all powered by OpenAI's large language models, fine-tuned with Upwork's proprietary data.

What Uma can do: 
→ Draft job posts from just a few keywords 
→ Explain why a freelancer is a strong match 
→ Evaluate thousands of profiles in seconds 
→ Deliver a ready-to-review shortlist automatically

There's also a ChatGPT × Upwork integration, brainstorm in ChatGPT and hand it off to Uma for talent matching.

On privacy: Your data is not used to train OpenAI's public models.

Let's be real:
Upwork is no longer a freelance marketplace. It's an AI-first, PPC-driven, fee-heavy platform that's pricing out good clients and pushing premium business to Lifted.

The old Upwork is dead.

The question is, are you going to keep playing a rigged game, or are you going to adapt your strategy?

How many times have you heard that 
AI is now screening your applications, 
scoring your LinkedIn profile, 
and deciding your fate before a recruiter even gets involved?

I spent some time exploring what companies are actually deploying, 
not what vendors are selling, 
not what influencers are warning you about, 

but what is genuinely in use and where.

Here's what the landscape actually looks like.

Tools like HireVue, Eightfold, and Pymetrics (the ones cited most often in the "AI is taking over recruitment" narrative) - start at $35,000 a year at the low end and scale into six and seven-figure annual contracts for large enterprises. 

Eightfold, one of the more sophisticated platforms, costs in the region of $7–$10 per employee per month. For a 10,000-person company, that's close to a million dollars a year.

That is not something your average mid-market employer, your regional logistics firm, or your local scale-up is running. Most of them are on Recruitee, Teamtailor, or Greenhouse - solid tools, but fundamentally workflow and tracking systems, not AI decision-makers.

The companies genuinely using AI-assisted screening are the ones with catastrophic volume problems. For eg:

A major corporate receiving 250,000 applications for 800 entry level vacancies. 
A global food chain hiring hundreds of restaurant staff across hundreds of locations simultaneously. 
Financial services graduate programmes processing tens of thousands of identical CVs from near-identical candidates.

For those contexts? Yes, AI is making the first cut. 
And honestly, it's a reasonable response to an otherwise unmanageable problem.

But here's what even those systems are doing: 
ranking and flagging, not rejecting. 
A human recruiter still reviews the output. 
The AI shortlists. 
It does not decide.

Paradox's Olivia - handles interview logistics for exactly this kind of high-volume hourly hiring. 
Retail. Warehousing. QSR. 
It asks "are you available weekends?" and books a slot. 
That is the full depth of its intelligence in most deployments. 
It is a decision tree with a friendly face, not a judgment system.

For the skilled, mid-career market most of you are in? 
A person is reading your application. 

The problem isn't the algorithm - it's everything that happens before and after that moment.

We'll soon talk about it.

I was talking to a friend and investor in New Jersey on the disrupting impact of #AI in particularly legal field and he was of the view that legal profession is in grave danger from AI. 

Obviously I didn't agree with it.

For over a decade, investing at the earliest stages, I’ve learned to question one default assumption:
“Efficiency will shrink the profession.”

History says otherwise.

You could argue AI compresses legal fees even as volume grows, squeezing the middle of the market.

That’s true. But incomplete.

Because what follows is predictable: new specialisations, new entrants, higher total spend.

This is Jevons paradox in motion: efficiency drives demand.

Law is now entering that phase.

➡️Verification rebound

AI lowers the cost of asking legal questions, but not the cost of being wrong.
The American Bar Association reports ~1.375M lawyers in 2025, alongside strong hiring.

Meanwhile, 78% of companies are bringing work in-house, and hiring more lawyers to audit AI outputs.

Until liability for AI outputs is settled law, humans remain the accountable layer.

➡️Complexity explosion

AI makes drafting cheap.
It makes review harder.
Contracts are longer. Edge cases are deeper.
A 2026 Legartis report shows law firm costs up 8.6%, despite AI.
We’re moving to infinite drafts, finite trust.

➡️Regulatory expansion
AI isn’t just a tool, it’s a new domain of law.
From the EU AI Act to India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act, compliance is exploding.
AI ethics, data rights, IP litigation, entire categories are being created.

➡️Composition shift (the real disruption)

Will some roles disappear? Yes.
Pure-play document review at the junior end will compress.

But that’s a task-level disruption, not a profession-level one.

What replaces it:
AI auditors.
Tech-law specialists.
In-house legal operators embedded with product and data teams.

Fewer repetitive roles. More judgment-heavy ones.

The mistake is linear thinking.

AI will automate parts of legal work.
But it will expand the surface area of law even faster.

This isn't replacement. It’s re-composition at the micro level, and expansion at the macro level.

The real question is:
 Are we preparing lawyers for the work that’s disappearing?

 Or the work that’s being created? Drop your views in the comments

I’ve been noticing a pattern.
Someone builds a solid career inside a company, often at a certain scale, in a well-defined role. Then something happens. Layoff, restructuring, or they get fired. The next step feels obvious: “I’ll start my own company.”

On paper, it makes sense. You take the skill that made you valuable and apply it independently. If you were successful before, why wouldn’t it work again?

Because the job you had is not the same as running a business.

Inside a company, a large part of what makes you effective is invisible support. HR handles contracts and hiring. Finance manages budgets, cash flow, and compliance. Marketing generates demand. Leadership sets direction. Operations absorb friction. You are operating within a system designed to let you focus.

When you go solo, that system disappears.
Now you are the system.

You are doing the work, but also pricing it, selling it, negotiating it, invoicing it, chasing payments, handling taxes, dealing with legal, and constantly managing uncertainty. Even if each individual task is manageable, the combination is what changes the game.

I often see another assumption layered on top: “I’ll figure out the missing pieces with AI.” It can help, but it doesn’t replace judgment, experience, or accountability across multiple functions. Tools don’t eliminate complexity, they just shift how you engage with it.

This is not to discourage anyone from starting a company. It’s one of the most rewarding and interesting moves you can make in your career.

But it’s not a linear extension of your previous role. It’s a fundamentally different problem.

As a freelancer, I sometimes get approached by early-stage founders who are transitioning directly from employment in an industry. I tend to be cautious, not because of their expertise, but because the risk is usually underestimated.

Being good at a function is not the same as building a business around it.

Here's what quietly changed in the legal backdrop in 2026, and why HR teams need to pay attention:

• The Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 came fully into force on 5 February 2026. It replaced Article 22's blanket prohibition on automated decision-making with a legitimate-interests framework.

• The ICO's updated AI and Biometrics Strategy explicitly states they'll scrutinise the use of automated decision-making in recruitment in 2026, looking specifically at transparency, discrimination and redress.

• The ICO consultation on draft ADM guidance runs until 29 May 2026, with updated recruitment and selection guidance landing later this year.

• The Equality Act 2010 remains the sharpest risk. AI doesn't get a pass — if a tool produces a discriminatory outcome, the employer is liable.

The trap most SMBs walk into isn't malicious. It's this: you buy an AI screening tool, it works "well enough", and six months later you've made 80 hiring decisions with no documented human review, no Data Protection Impact Assessment, and no way to explain to a candidate why they were screened out.

A seven-point audit to run before the end of this quarter:

1. Map every AI touchpoint in your hiring funnel — including CV screening, video interview analysis, and chatbots
2. Run a DPIA before you deploy, not after an ICO letter arrives
3. Ask your vendor whose CVs the model was trained on
4. Define what "meaningful human review" actually looks like in your process
5. Write candidate-facing transparency notices
6. Test for adverse impact across protected characteristics
7. Set a retraining and monitoring cadence — at least quarterly

AI in recruitment is lawful in the UK. Careless AI in recruitment isn't. The difference is mostly documentation — and whether anyone has actually read the vendor contract.

Three conversations this week - one with a programme director in Dubai, one with a hiring manager in Riyadh, one with a PM who just had an offer withdrawn. All three told me something different about the same market.

Here is what the data says when you put all three together.

GCC hired 1% more in Q1 2026 while Oxford Economics just downgraded the region's GDP growth to -0.2%.
Those two numbers tell you everything about what is happening in this job market right now.

The Iran conflict did not kill hiring in the GCC. It filtered it.
The roles that paused were generic. The roles still open are specific and the specifications have quietly changed in ways most PMOs and PMs have not noticed yet.

Here is what I am observing on the ground:
1. RFP and procurement experience is now a baseline, not a bonus.
Job descriptions that once said "stakeholder management" now say "lead preparation of RFPs, RFQs, and tender documentation." PMOs that only know how to govern delivery are being passed over for PMOs that can also structure the commercial vehicle that funds it. 

2. Contract management has moved into the PMO function.
Roles that sat in commercial or legal teams 24 months ago are now embedded in programme governance. Parsing FIDIC clauses, managing variation orders, handling contractor claims these are appearing in senior PMO job descriptions across UAE and KSA. This is not scope creep. This is the market repricing what a senior PMO lead is worth.

3. AI tool experience is being screened but not how most people think.
Microsoft Copilot has 15 million paid enterprise seats globally. Only 35.8% of those users actively use it. That gap is your differentiator not the licence, but the proof of what you did with it. "Used Copilot" on a CV scores nothing. "Reduced programme reporting time by 40% using Copilot-integrated Power BI dashboards, freeing 12 hours of PMO capacity per week" - that gets a call.

4. Value realisation after go-live is now a front-end buying criterion.
Clients are no longer separating implementation from outcomes. They want the PM who can define what value looks like before the project starts - and prove it was delivered after it closes. If your experience stops at go-live, you are covering 80% of the delivery lifecycle and pricing yourself as though you covered 100%.

The conflict created a talent gap, not a talent surplus. Governments are not slowing down their national agendas. If anything, execution pressure is increasing and the talent that left or paused creates a window for senior practitioners with regional delivery credibility to move faster than the noise suggests.

The market is not closed. It is recalibrated.

What shift have you seen in your own job search or hiring process over the last 90 days? Genuinely curious what others are observing.

Let me say the quiet part out loud:
In about 18 months, an ordinary person with an AI agent on their phone will be able to do, in minutes, what takes a seasoned specialist most of a day today.

Think-
Consulting.
Software.
Tech support.
Operations.

Not because people suddenly became exceptional.
Because intelligence is being industrialized.
Every codebase, user manual, report, meeting note, Slack thread, decision log, and forgotten workaround is becoming machine-readable memory.
And memory, at that scale, stops acting like a library.
It starts acting like a second workforce. A second brain!
That is the shift most people still underestimate.

AI began as a tool that needed context from us.
Soon, it will return the context to us.
Not just answering.
Not just generating.
But reminding, connecting, drafting, escalating, following up, and reviving the task that slipped through the cracks three Tuesdays ago.

The missed action item.
The buried customer promise.
The bug nobody reopened.
The idea that almost lived.
This is where it stops feeling like software and starts feeling like a necessary evil.
Because once AI can remember better than us, retrieve faster than us, pattern-match wider than us, and produce artifacts in our own style, knowledge and the intelligence to apply stops being leverage.

And for centuries, that was the bargain.
Society rewarded people for what they knew, how well they could apply it, and how long it took them to earn that usefulness.
Years of study.
Years of scar tissue.
Years of becoming valuable.
Now machines can imitate the appearance of all three.

That is the real rupture.
Not convenience.
Not productivity.
Not even automation.
A rupture in the social contract.
Because if knowledge becomes cheap, and applied intelligence becomes abundant, what exactly is human value priced on next?

That is why AI anxiety feels so visceral.
People are not just afraid of new tools.
They can feel their accumulated leverage being marked down in real time.

White-collar work feels it first.
Software especially.
Vocational work may hold longer because the physical world is still stubborn.

A pipe still leaks in three dimensions.
A car still refuses diagnosis with attitude.
Wood still has grain, weight, resistance, mood.
But even that grace period shrinks when humanoids become commonplace.

And once institutions believe labor itself can be substituted at scale, they do not just redesign jobs.
They redesign education.
Hiring.
Policy.
Power.

That is the darker truth underneath all this:
AI is not only changing work.
It is renegotiating what a human being is worth in the market.
The winners will not be the people who know the most.
They will be the people who can think with the machine without slowly becoming one.

Because when memory is infinite and competence is rented, what remains rare is judgment, taste, courage, and a soul that still sounds human.

-Prateek

Contract labor can protect access in the short term. It cannot carry the workforce model forever.

That is the uncomfortable truth many healthcare organizations are already living. The American Hospital Association reported that hospitals’ labor costs increased by more than $42.5 billion between 2021 and 2023 to reach $839 billion, accounting for nearly 60% of the average hospital’s expenses. It also reported that hospitals spent approximately $51.1 billion on contracted staff in 2023 to fill gaps and maintain access to care. Those figures make it clear that contract labor is not being used as a small tactical supplement. It has become a costly response to structural instability in the workforce system. (American Hospital Association)

That does not mean contract labor is unnecessary. In many environments it is an essential shock absorber. It keeps units open, fills sudden gaps, supports seasonal pressure, and gives organizations time they might not otherwise have. But that is exactly the point: it buys time. It does not solve the underlying reason that time had to be bought in the first place.
The market should be more candid about that.

Heavy reliance on premium staffing often signals deeper flaws—slow permanent hiring, fragmented workflows, poor candidate movement, weak readiness design, or high preventable turnover. Contract labor then becomes the visible cost of invisible inefficiency.

That’s why it’s dangerous to mistake the shock absorber for the engine. A stronger strategy asks: how often must we rely on premium labor? The answer usually lies upstream—faster permanent hiring, better recruiter leverage, smoother candidate movement, earlier trust and readiness handling, and lower workflow friction.

This is where Gigin Health fits more naturally than a generic AI hiring tool would. Its positioning is built around connected healthcare hiring operations: a healthcare ATS plus agentic suite, Gia handling front-end recruiting load, a workflow from application to onboarding, and healthcare-specific support for repetitive tasks and credential-oriented process steps. In practical terms, that points toward exactly the kind of workflow compression organizations need if they want to reduce reliance on high-cost labor substitutes over time. (gigin.health)

The strategic goal is not to eliminate contract labor completely. That would be unrealistic. The goal is to make it less necessary as a response to preventable process weakness.
Contract labor should remain a tactical instrument. It should not become the business model the hiring system quietly depends on.

Healthcare organizations and staffing teams looking to strengthen permanent hiring workflows and reduce avoidable labor friction can explore Gigin.Health via gigin.ai or write to info@gigin.ai.

At the enterprise level, things are moving. Large organisations are formalising AI leadership, forming partnerships, and rolling out tools. Some have appointed Chief AI Officers. And this is not only a US story. In the EU, business use of AI has risen sharply in the past two years, and Nordic firms are already piloting generative AI and hiring experts to use it systematically.

But having an AI strategy is not the same as having AI leadership at every level.
Here is what I think is being underestimated: the people closest to the work, the formal and informal leaders, have more power to shape this transition than they realise.

Enterprise AI strategy may be decided centrally. But whether AI actually changes how work gets done, and whether people feel supported through that change, is often decided one team at a time.

You may not set enterprise policy. But you already shape how your team learns, how they experiment, where AI adds value and where human judgment stays essential. And most importantly, whether your people feel prepared for what is coming or anxious about it.

That is not a technology question. It is a leadership question.

The leaders who will stand out in this transition are not the ones waiting for perfect clarity from above. They are the ones who make the shift tangible for their teams, through clear principles, thoughtful experimentation, and human judgment about where AI helps and where it should not lead.
In this transition, leadership cannot stay implicit.

#AI #Leadership #AITransformation #FutureOfWork
